---
title: "Validation"
subtitle: "Closed form vs Monte Carlo"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: right
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

Each [Model](model.qmd) identity is z-tested against a Monte Carlo
run. You can regenerate the run with `npm run simulate -- --seed 42
&& npm run sweep -- --seed 42`.

The z-score $z = (\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{mc}}[\Pi] -
\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{cf}}[\Pi]) / \mathrm{stderr}$ standardises the
MC mean against its closed-form target. We treat $|z| \le 2$ as
sampling noise and $|z| > 3$ as suspect. Three identities are exact
rather than statistical. The ratio
$\mathrm{Var}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] / \mathrm{Var}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}] =
1 / f^2$ is algebraic. The matched-desk identity $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}
+ \mathrm{treasury}(N P, N P S_0) = N(Q - P S_0)$ holds path by
path. The retained SD identity $\mathrm{SD}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}] =
(1 - \beta) \mathrm{SD}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ is an algebraic
consequence of $\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}} = (1 - \beta) \Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}
+ \pi_{\mathrm{syn}}$. The full symbol and risk-metric definitions
sit in [Setup and notation](model.qmd#setup). The sections below
draw from `data/run-42.json`.


```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import { formatTickCurrency } from "./lib/compiled/src/core/index.js"
```

## Run parameters

The cards below show the parameters of the fixed Monte Carlo run
that the rest of this page validates against. The price process is
specified by $(\mu, \sigma, T)$, the throughput by $\lambda$, and
the business levers by $(f, Q, \alpha)$. The last card reports the
[break-even quote](model.qmd#comparison) $Q^*$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (coverage)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

## Per-book scorecard

The table below has one row per stochastic book and one column per
statistic; closed-form and Monte Carlo results sit side by side.
The switching row has no closed-form mean or SD, because its P&L
density has no closed form. The four mean-level anchors for the
switching book — the first-passage probability
$\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$, the expected first-passage time
$\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$, the expected time in fee mode
$\mathbb{E}[T_{\mathrm{fee}}]$, and the expected fee-mode spot
integral $\mathbb{E}[I_{\mathrm{fee}}]$ — are tested further down.

<div class="legend-table">

$\Pi$ is the row book's terminal P&L in USD over $[0, T]$.

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (cf) / (mc) | closed-form / MC mean |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | $1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD} / \sqrt{n}$ |
| SD (cf) / (mc) | closed-form / MC SD |
| $z$ | $(\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{mc}} - \mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{cf}}) / \mathrm{stderr}$ |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | 5% / 1% loss thresholds |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | tail means |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ | loss probability |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$ (horizon-absolute) |

</div>

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "book",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "n/a" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

A z-score in the $\pm 2$ range is sampling noise. A b2b row whose
MC variance equals $1 / f^2$ times the fee MC variance confirms the
algebraic identity between the two books.

## Desk compositions

The table below shows how the operating books and the treasury
combine into full desks. The `matched` desk $= N(Q - P S_0)$ is
deterministic, so its closed-form and MC values coincide. The
`partial_desk` at `run.params.alpha` uses the $J_\alpha$ closed
form. The `syndicated_matched` desk $= \mathrm{retained} +
\mathrm{treasury}(N P, N P S_0)$ is still deterministic up to the
premium. The `switching_matched` desk $= \mathrm{switching} +
\mathrm{treasury}(N P, N P S_0)$ is reported from Monte Carlo only.
The `switching_partial_desk` $= \mathrm{switching} +
\mathrm{treasury}(\alpha N P, \alpha N P S_0)$ pairs the switching
operating book with the $\alpha$-partial treasury at
`run.params.alpha`; MC only.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
deskRows = [
  {
    name: "matched",
    closedFormMean: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    closedFormSd: run.desks.matched.closedFormSd,
    mcMean: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    mcSd: 0,
    mcCi95: 0,
    zScore: 0,
    var95: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    var99: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    cvar95: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    cvar99: -run.desks.matched.closedFormMean,
    probLoss: run.desks.matched.closedFormMean < 0 ? 1 : 0,
    sharpe: null,
  },
  run.desks.partial,
  run.desks.syndicatedMatched,
  ...(run.desks.switchingMatched ? [run.desks.switchingMatched] : []),
  ...(run.desks.switchingPartial ? [run.desks.switchingPartial] : []),
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(deskRows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "desk",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => Number.isFinite(x) ? (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2) : "n/a",
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => Number.isFinite(x) ? "$" + x.toFixed(2) : "n/a",
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "n/a" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

The `matched` row confirms the deterministic identity $N(Q - P S_0)$
to machine precision. The `partial_desk` row tracks the $J_\alpha$
closed form to within sampling noise.

## Distribution checks

The three sub-sections below move from moment-level checks on the
Merton compensation to full empirical distributions of per-book
P&L and of the driving integral $I_T$.

### Mean invariance under Merton

With the [Merton compensation](model.qmd#jumps) in place, means
match pure GBM exactly and variances grow. The canonical overlay
used here is $\lambda_J = 3$/yr, $\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$.
We expect $|z| \lesssim 2$ on the means and $\mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{merton}}
> \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{gbm}}$ on the variances.

The table below compares the closed-form GBM prediction to a Monte
Carlo run that includes the jump overlay, one row per book. The
columns report both means, both SDs, the MC 95 % confidence
interval, and the z-score of the jump-aware MC mean against the
pure-GBM closed form.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.jumpCheck.rows, {
  columns: ["name","gbmClosedMean","mertonMcMean","mertonMcCi95","gbmClosedSd","mertonMcSd","zVsGbmClosed"],
  header: {
    name: "book",
    gbmClosedMean: "E[Π] gbm-cf",
    mertonMcMean: "E[Π] merton-mc",
    mertonMcCi95: "±CI95",
    gbmClosedSd: "SD gbm-cf",
    mertonMcSd: "SD merton-mc",
    zVsGbmClosed: "z vs gbm-cf"
  },
  format: {
    gbmClosedMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    gbmClosedSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mertonMcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zVsGbmClosed: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
  }
})
```

_Overlay from `JUMP_CHECK` in the CLI._

Means agree within sampling noise, and Merton SDs exceed GBM SDs on
every book; this is the jump variance overlay at work.

The card below spot-checks the terminal-price identity
$\mathbb{E}[S_T] = S_0 e^{\mu T}$, which should hold for every
$(\lambda_J, \mu_J, \sigma_J)$ under the Merton compensation.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] gbm closed</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.gbmClosed.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] merton mc</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.mertonMcMean.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">z vs gbm closed</div><div class="value">${(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed).toFixed(2)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### P&L distributions

The panels below show one empirical histogram per stochastic book;
the x-axis is terminal P&L in USD, and the y-axis is empirical
density. Each panel gets its own x-domain clipped to the
$[0.5\%, 99.5\%]$ quantile window of that book's samples, so a
heavy Merton tail in one book does not squash the others onto a
single bin. The b2b, retained, and partial-desk panels use a
symmetric-log x-axis to keep their wider tails legible. The black
rule is the closed-form mean; the dashed grey rule sits at
$\Pi = 0$, so the mass to its left is $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Matched desk omitted (deterministic spike). Switching row present
// only when the fixture carries a finite barrier.
pnlBooks = [
  "fee", "b2b", "retained", "treasury", "partial_desk",
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switching ? ["switching"] : []),
  ...(run.sampleTraces.switchingPartialDesk ? ["switching_partial_desk"] : []),
]

// Closed-form means: operating rows from run.rows, partial desk from
// run.desks.partial. Switching-partial desk has no closed form; use MC mean.
meanByBook = {
  const rows = run.rows.filter(r => pnlBooks.includes(r.name));
  const m = new Map(rows.map(r => [r.name, Number.isFinite(r.closedFormMean) ? r.closedFormMean : r.mcMean]));
  m.set("partial_desk", run.desks.partial.closedFormMean);
  if (run.desks.switchingPartial) {
    m.set("switching_partial_desk", run.desks.switchingPartial.mcMean);
  }
  return m;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Per-book panel helper (mirrors the Simulator page). Each panel has
// its own quantile-clipped x-domain and Freedman–Diaconis binning;
// wide-tailed books use a symmetric-log x-axis so their tails stay
// readable next to the bulk.
pnlPanel = {
  function quantileSorted(sorted, p) {
    const n = sorted.length;
    if (n === 0) return NaN;
    if (n === 1) return sorted[0];
    const pos = p * (n - 1);
    const lo = Math.floor(pos), hi = Math.ceil(pos);
    if (lo === hi) return sorted[lo];
    return sorted[lo] * (hi - pos) + sorted[hi] * (pos - lo);
  }
  function sdOf(samples) {
    const n = samples.length;
    if (n < 2) return 0;
    let sum = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) sum += samples[i];
    const mu = sum / n;
    let sse = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
      const d = samples[i] - mu;
      sse += d * d;
    }
    return Math.sqrt(sse / (n - 1));
  }
  return function ({ label, samples, mean, color, symlog, qLo = 0.005, qHi = 0.995 }) {
    const n = samples.length;
    if (n === 0) return null;
    const sorted = Float64Array.from(samples).sort();
    const qWindowLo = quantileSorted(sorted, qLo);
    const qWindowHi = quantileSorted(sorted, qHi);
    const q25 = quantileSorted(sorted, 0.25);
    const q75 = quantileSorted(sorted, 0.75);
    const iqr = q75 - q25;
    const sd = sdOf(samples);
    let xMin = Math.min(0, qWindowLo);
    let xMax = Math.max(0, qWindowHi);
    if (xMax - xMin < 1e-9) {
      const pad = Math.max(1, Math.abs(mean) * 0.1);
      xMin = mean - pad;
      xMax = mean + pad;
    }
    let clippedLo = 0, clippedHi = 0;
    const inWindow = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
      const v = samples[i];
      if (v < xMin) clippedLo++;
      else if (v > xMax) clippedHi++;
      else inWindow.push(v);
    }
    const fdWidth = iqr > 0 ? 2 * iqr / Math.cbrt(n) : 0;
    const thresholds = fdWidth > 0
      ? Math.min(120, Math.max(20, Math.ceil((xMax - xMin) / fdWidth)))
      : 40;
    const xConfig = {
      label: "terminal P&L Π (USD)",
      tickFormat: formatTickCurrency,
      domain: [xMin, xMax],
      nice: false,
    };
    if (symlog) {
      xConfig.type = "symlog";
      xConfig.constant = Math.max(1, iqr / 2, sd / 4);
    }
    const plot = Plot.plot({
      width: 900,
      height: 170,
      marginLeft: 70,
      marginRight: 20,
      marginBottom: 36,
      marginTop: 10,
      grid: true,
      x: xConfig,
      y: { label: "density", ticks: 3 },
      marks: [
        Plot.ruleY([0]),
        Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
        Plot.rectY(inWindow, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, {
          x: d => d, fill: color, thresholds,
        })),
        Plot.ruleX([mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
      ],
    });
    const wrap = document.createElement("div");
    wrap.className = "pnl-panel";
    const header = document.createElement("div");
    header.className = "pnl-panel-title";
    const name = document.createElement("span");
    name.className = "pnl-panel-name";
    name.textContent = label;
    header.appendChild(name);
    if (symlog) {
      const tag = document.createElement("span");
      tag.className = "pnl-panel-tag";
      tag.textContent = "symlog x";
      header.appendChild(tag);
    }
    wrap.appendChild(header);
    wrap.appendChild(plot);
    const clippedPct = ((clippedLo + clippedHi) / n) * 100;
    const footer = document.createElement("div");
    footer.className = "pnl-panel-footer";
    footer.textContent = clippedPct > 0
      ? `window: ${(qLo * 100).toFixed(1)}%–${(qHi * 100).toFixed(1)}% quantile · ${clippedPct.toFixed(2)}% of ${n.toLocaleString()} paths outside (${clippedLo} below, ${clippedHi} above)`
      : `window: ${(qLo * 100).toFixed(1)}%–${(qHi * 100).toFixed(1)}% quantile · all ${n.toLocaleString()} paths inside`;
    wrap.appendChild(footer);
    return wrap;
  };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Books with wide Merton tails use symlog; fee / treasury / switching
// are narrow enough that linear panels read better.
pnlSymlog = new Set(["b2b", "retained", "partial_desk", "switching_partial_desk"])
pnlColors = {
  "fee": "#4c78a8",
  "b2b": "#f58518",
  "retained": "#e45756",
  "treasury": "#72b7b2",
  "partial_desk": "#54a24b",
  "switching": "#b279a2",
  "switching_partial_desk": "#ff9da6",
}

pnlPanelsNode = {
  const specs = [
    { label: "fee", samples: run.sampleTraces.fee },
    { label: "b2b", samples: run.sampleTraces.b2b },
    { label: "retained", samples: run.sampleTraces.retained },
    { label: "treasury", samples: run.sampleTraces.treasury },
    { label: "partial_desk", samples: run.sampleTraces.partialDesk },
    ...(run.sampleTraces.switching
      ? [{ label: "switching", samples: run.sampleTraces.switching }]
      : []),
    ...(run.sampleTraces.switchingPartialDesk
      ? [{ label: "switching_partial_desk", samples: run.sampleTraces.switchingPartialDesk }]
      : []),
  ];
  const grid = document.createElement("div");
  grid.className = "pnl-panels";
  for (const spec of specs) {
    const node = pnlPanel({
      ...spec,
      mean: meanByBook.get(spec.label),
      color: pnlColors[spec.label] ?? "#4c78a8",
      symlog: pnlSymlog.has(spec.label),
    });
    if (node) grid.appendChild(node);
  }
  return grid;
}
```

_Black: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey: $\Pi = 0$.
Panels labelled "symlog x" use a symmetric-log x-scale with a
linear region around zero; the others are linear._

The black rule lands inside every histogram hump, confirming that
the closed-form means match the empirical distributions.

### The $I_T$ histogram

The histogram below shows the Monte Carlo distribution of the spot
integral $I_T = \int_0^T S_t \, dt$. The solid black rule marks the
Dufresne mean $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$, and the dashed black rules mark
$\pm 1$ SD around it. $I_T$ is not log-normal, so tail quantiles
still require Monte Carlo.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Black: $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$. Dashed: $\pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$._

The empirical histogram is visibly asymmetric and sits to the right
of the mean, which is the lognormal skew of $I_T$ coming through.

## Matched treasury

The matched treasury has deterministic terminal P&L by
construction, but its interim NAV path and intra-horizon drawdown
are the funding-risk profile the shape hides.

### NAV over time

Terminal P&L of the matched treasury is deterministic, but the
interim NAV $V_t = N P (1 - t/T) S_t$ is a scaled GBM that pins to
zero at $T$ and can dip before then. The chart below plots $V_t$
against normalised time $t / T$, with 20 sampled paths in grey and
the first path highlighted in orange.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx,
    series: idx === 0 ? "first path" : "sampled paths",
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  color: {
    legend: true,
    domain: ["sampled paths", "first path"],
    range: ["#888", "#c94"],
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id !== 0), { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "series", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "series", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_$V_T = 0$ regardless of $S_T$._

All paths terminate at zero because the $(1 - t/T)$ factor kills
the NAV at $T$, but several paths dip well below zero mid-horizon.

### Shortfall

We define the worst interim NAV drawdown as

$$
D := \max_{t \le T} \bigl[ N P (1 - t/T) (S_0 - S_t) \bigr]_+.
$$

The histogram below counts Monte Carlo paths by their value of $D$;
the two black rules mark the 95th and 99th percentiles (dashed and
solid respectively).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed: 95th. Solid: 99th._

The right tail is the intra-horizon funding need the matched
treasury exposes, even though its terminal P&L is zero by
construction.

## Parameter sweep {#parameter-sweep}

We fix the partial-desk composition and sweep the drift, volatility,
and coverage fraction $(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ over a grid. The data
comes from `data/sweep.json`. The two sliders below select a slice
of the grid.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "coverage α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: -0.1, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility $\sigma$

The chart below plots the 95 % tail-mean loss
$\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ of the partial desk, in USD, against the
annualised volatility $\sigma$ at the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of partial desk   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Partial-desk CVaR₉₅ vs $\sigma$._

CVaR₉₅ grows monotonically with $\sigma$, as expected since higher
volatility fattens the loss tail of the uncovered window.

### Tail risk vs coverage $\alpha$

The chart below plots the same tail-mean loss against the coverage
$\alpha$, with volatility fixed at the second grid value. CVaR
decays with $\alpha$, and at $\alpha = 1$ the desk is deterministic,
so CVaR₉₅ collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Coverage α vs tail risk on the partial desk. σ fixed at the second grid value. At α = 1 the desk is deterministic so CVaR₉₅ collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "coverage α   (0 = b2b only, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of partial desk   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partialDesk.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## Syndication

The formulae for syndication live in [Syndicating the back-to-back
book](model.qmd#syndication). At $\theta = 0$, $\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}$
is an affine rescaling of $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$ with slope
$(1 - \beta)$, so the pricing is closed form and no extra Monte
Carlo is needed. The `cvar`-mode factor
$\phi(\Phi^{-1}(0.95))/0.05 \approx 2.063$ puts the `sharpe` and
`cvar` loads on a common SD scale. The three sliders below control
the slice we visualise.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickSigma = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.sigmas, { value: sweep.grid.sigmas[1], label: "volatility σ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickTheta = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.05, value: 0, label: "counterparty load θ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMode = Inputs.radio(["sharpe", "cvar"], { value: "sharpe", label: "premium mode" })
```

### Retained CVaR₉₅ across $\beta$

The chart below plots the retained book's 95 % tail-mean loss
against the cession fraction $\beta$. The curve collapses linearly
in $(1 - \beta)$, and a positive counterparty load $\theta$ lifts
it by $\beta \rho(\theta)$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
gaussianCvar95 = 2.062713055949736
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
betaGrid = Array.from({ length: 21 }, (_, i) => i / 20)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
retainedCvarCurves = {
  // Π_ret = (1 − β)·Π_b2b + π_loaded.
  // CVaR95(Π_ret) = (1 − β)·cvar95(b2b) − β·mean(b2b) + β·θ·loadFactor·sd(b2b).
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const rows = [];
  const cell = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell) return rows;
  const b2bMean = cell.b2b.mean;
  const b2bCvar = cell.b2b.cvar95;
  const b2bSd = cell.b2b.sd;
  for (const beta of betaGrid) {
    const fairCvar = (1 - beta) * b2bCvar - beta * b2bMean;
    const loadShift = beta * pickTheta * loadFactor * b2bSd;
    rows.push({ beta, cvar: fairCvar + loadShift });
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Retained CVaR₉₅ as β sweeps 0→1. Linear in (1 − β); θ > 0 shifts up.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of retained   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(retainedCvarCurves, { x: "beta", y: "cvar", stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

The straight line is the $(1 - \beta)$ rescaling of the b2b tail
translated into CVaR space; at full cession $\beta = 1$ the tail
sits at the negative of the mean b2b P&L, ceded away.

### Fair vs loaded premium

The chart below plots the premium the intermediary receives as a
function of the cession fraction $\beta$, with one curve per
counterparty load $\theta$. At $\theta = 0$ the premium is
actuarially fair, $\pi_{\mathrm{fair}} = \beta
\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$. At $\theta > 0$ the load pulls the
premium down by $\beta \rho(\theta)$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
premiumCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const piFair = beta * cell0.b2b.mean;
      const piLoaded = piFair - beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, pi: piLoaded, isFair: theta === 0 });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Premium vs β at a handful of θ. θ = 0: actuarially fair.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "premium π   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(premiumCurves, { x: "beta", y: "pi", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Expected-P&L cost of syndication

The chart below plots the expected-P&L shift $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}]
- \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ against the cession fraction
$\beta$, again with one curve per counterparty load $\theta$. At
$\theta = 0$ the curve is flat, so cession is a free lunch in
expectation. At $\theta > 0$ the load drags the expected P&L down
linearly in $\beta$: the intermediary pays the counterparty the
load in exchange for tail relief.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
meanShiftCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const shift = -beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, shift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Cost of syndication vs β. Flat at θ = 0.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "E[Π_ret] − E[Π_b2b]   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(meanShiftCurves, { x: "beta", y: "shift", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

## Switching

The formulae live in [Switching to fee above a
threshold](model.qmd#switching). The switching P&L density has no
closed form, so Monte Carlo is authoritative on the tails. Four
pure-GBM mean anchors are still z-testable: the first-passage
probability $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$, the expected first-passage
time $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$, the expected time in fee mode
$\mathbb{E}[T_{\mathrm{fee}}]$, and the expected fee-mode spot
integral $\mathbb{E}[I_{\mathrm{fee}}]$. Jumps change the
$\tau$ distribution, so under jumps those anchors fall back to
Monte Carlo too.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingSweep = FileAttachment("data/switching-sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingRows = switchingSweep.cells.map(c => ({
  h: c.h === null ? Infinity : c.h,
  hLabel: c.h === null ? "∞ (no switch)" : c.h.toFixed(2),
  mean: c.row.mean,
  cvar95: c.row.cvar95,
  cvar99: c.row.cvar99,
  probLoss: c.row.probLoss,
  probSwitchMc: c.switching ? c.switching.probSwitch.mc : 0,
  probSwitchCf: c.switching?.probSwitch?.closedForm ?? null,
  expectedTauMc: c.switching ? c.switching.expectedCrossingTime.mc : switchingSweep.base.T,
  expectedTauCf: c.switching?.expectedCrossingTime?.closedForm ?? null,
  expectedTauZ: c.switching?.expectedCrossingTime?.zScore ?? null,
  meanTimeInFeeMc: c.switching ? c.switching.meanTimeInFee.mc : 0,
  meanTimeInFeeCf: c.switching?.meanTimeInFee?.closedForm ?? null,
  meanTimeInFeeZ: c.switching?.meanTimeInFee?.zScore ?? null,
  meanIntegralFeeMc: c.switching ? c.switching.meanIntegralFee.mc : 0,
  meanIntegralFeeCf: c.switching?.meanIntegralFee?.closedForm ?? null,
  meanIntegralFeeZ: c.switching?.meanIntegralFee?.zScore ?? null,
  meanNCrossings: c.switching?.meanNCrossings ?? 0,
  meanFracInFeeMode: c.switching ? c.switching.meanFracInFeeMode : 0,
  retainedMean: c.retained.mean,
  retainedCvar95: c.retained.cvar95,
  feeMean: c.fee.mean,
  feeCvar95: c.fee.cvar95,
}))
```

### Mean and tail vs threshold

The first chart below shows how the 95 % tail-mean loss changes as
the threshold $h$ falls from the no-switch limit, and the second
shows the same for the expected P&L. Solid coloured line: the
switching book. Dashed grey rule: the retained anchor at $h = \infty$.
As $h$ falls, the tail loss drops from the b2b reference toward
fee-book levels and the mean rises. Paths that re-enter below the
threshold limit how far the cut goes.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 60,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Switching CVaR₉₅ vs h. Dashed: retained anchor.",
  x: { label: "threshold h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of switching book   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", stroke: "#a33b5b", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", fill: "#a33b5b", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? 0, t: "retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "E[Π] vs h.",
  x: { label: "threshold h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "E[Π] (switching)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: formatTickCurrency },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", stroke: "#2a7", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", fill: "#2a7", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? 0, t: "retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

Tightening $h$ trades expected P&L for a tighter tail, but
re-entries limit how far the tail can be cut.

### Pure-GBM anchors

The table below tests the four pure-GBM mean anchors, one row per
threshold $h$. Closed-form values come from `src/core/moments.ts`,
MC values come from the switching sweep, and z-scores compare the
two. A small discrete-crossing bias in $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ and
$\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$ shrinks as $n_{\mathrm{steps}}$ grows.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "probSwitchMc", "probSwitchCf",
              "expectedTauMc", "expectedTauCf", "expectedTauZ",
              "meanTimeInFeeMc", "meanTimeInFeeCf", "meanTimeInFeeZ",
              "meanIntegralFeeMc", "meanIntegralFeeCf", "meanIntegralFeeZ"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      probSwitchMc: "P[τ≤T] (mc)",
      probSwitchCf: "P[τ≤T] (cf)",
      expectedTauMc: "E[τ∧T] (mc)",
      expectedTauCf: "E[τ∧T] (cf)",
      expectedTauZ: "z(τ)",
      meanTimeInFeeMc: "E[T_fee] (mc)",
      meanTimeInFeeCf: "E[T_fee] (cf)",
      meanTimeInFeeZ: "z(T_fee)",
      meanIntegralFeeMc: "E[I_fee] (mc)",
      meanIntegralFeeCf: "E[I_fee] (cf)",
      meanIntegralFeeZ: "z(I_fee)",
    },
    format: {
      probSwitchMc: x => x.toFixed(3),
      probSwitchCf: x => x === null ? "n/a" : x.toFixed(3),
      expectedTauMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      expectedTauCf: x => x === null ? "n/a" : x.toFixed(4),
      expectedTauZ: x => x === null ? "n/a" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
      meanTimeInFeeMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      meanTimeInFeeCf: x => x === null ? "n/a" : x.toFixed(4),
      meanTimeInFeeZ: x => x === null ? "n/a" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
      meanIntegralFeeMc: x => x.toFixed(4),
      meanIntegralFeeCf: x => x === null ? "n/a" : x.toFixed(4),
      meanIntegralFeeZ: x => x === null ? "n/a" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    }
  }
)
```

### Time and count in fee mode

The table below reports how often and how long the switching book
sits in fee mode, one row per threshold $h$. The expected
fee-mode time fraction $\mathbb{E}[T_{\mathrm{fee}}/T]$ decreases
with $h$. The expected crossing count `E[# crossings]` counts
$\mathrm{sign}(S - H)$ flips on the discretised path.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "meanFracInFeeMode", "meanNCrossings", "mean", "cvar95"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      meanFracInFeeMode: "E[T_fee/T]",
      meanNCrossings: "E[# crossings]",
      mean: "E[Π]",
      cvar95: "CVaR95",
    },
    format: {
      meanFracInFeeMode: x => x.toFixed(3),
      meanNCrossings: x => x.toFixed(3),
      mean: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
      cvar95: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    }
  }
)
```

## Break-even quote surface

The break-even quote $Q^*$ equalises the expected P&L of the fee
and b2b books. In dimensionless form it reads

$$
\frac{Q^*}{(1 + f) P S_0} = \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T}.
$$

The chart below plots this ratio against the horizon $T$, with one
coloured curve per drift $\mu$. The dashed grey rule at $1.0$ is
the $\mu \to 0$ limit.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_One curve per $\mu$._

A positive drift lifts the curve above the $\mu = 0$ line, and a
negative drift sits below it.
